learning_objectives: Analyze the political, economic, legal, and cultural dimensions of the international business environment
Identify key challenges facing MNCs in cross-border operations
Evaluate how country risk assessment informs business strategy
readings: World Bank, Doing Business Report (latest overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: International business operates at the intersection of economics, politics, law, and culture. Success requires understanding all four dimensions.
key_concepts: PESTLE analysis for international markets
Country risk assessment: political, economic, financial
Cultural dimensions: Hofstede, GLOBE, Trompenaars
Legal environments: common law, civil law, religious law, customary law
The liability of foreignness in international business
lecture_notes.demonstration: Country risk assessment exercise: evaluating market entry feasibility for three emerging markets

review.value: Framework for analyzing the Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, and Environmental factors affecting business in a foreign market
The additional costs foreign firms face when operating in a host country compared to local firms, arising from cultural distance, institutional differences, and discrimination

class_schedule.topic: Trade Policy and the WTO System
learning_objectives: Analyze the WTO's role in governing international trade
Evaluate the proliferation of regional trade agreements
Assess the impact of trade wars and protectionism on international business

lecture_notes.introduction: The rules-based trading system is under unprecedented strain. Understanding trade policy is essential for businesses operating across borders.
key_concepts: WTO principles: MFN, national treatment, reciprocity
Dispute settlement mechanism and its crisis
Regional trade agreements: EU single market, USMCA, RCEP, CPTPP
Trade remedies: anti-dumping, countervailing duties, safeguards
US-China trade war: causes, tools, and business implications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: how a specific trade dispute (e.g., Airbus-Boeing) affected supply chains and business strategy

review.value: WTO principle requiring that any trade advantage given to one member must be extended to all members, preventing discriminatory bilateral deals
Preferential trade arrangements between groups of countries that go beyond WTO commitments, now numbering over 300 and covering the majority of world trade

class_schedule.topic: Foreign Direct Investment and Market Entry Strategies
learning_objectives: Compare different modes of international market entry
Analyze FDI determinants: OLI paradigm and location advantages
Evaluate the strategic choices between exporting, licensing, JVs, and wholly-owned subsidiaries
readings: Dunning, 'The Eclectic Paradigm of International Production'

lecture_notes.introduction: How a company enters a foreign market is one of its most consequential strategic decisions. The choice depends on industry, country, and firm-specific factors.
key_concepts: Entry modes: exporting, licensing, franchising, JV, wholly-owned subsidiary
OLI paradigm: ownership, location, and internalization advantages
Greenfield vs. acquisition entry
Political risk and investment protection: BITs and ISDS
Host country policies: investment incentives vs. performance requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Market entry strategy exercise: recommending entry mode for a company entering three different markets

review.value: Dunning's framework: firms invest abroad when they have Ownership advantages (technology, brand), Location advantages (market access, resources), and Internalization advantages (keeping control vs. licensing)
Agreements between two countries providing legal protections for foreign investors, including fair treatment, expropriation compensation, and access to international arbitration

class_schedule.topic: Global Supply Chain Management and Risk
learning_objectives: Analyze the structure and governance of global supply chains
Evaluate supply chain risks: geopolitical, pandemic, climate, cyber
Assess reshoring, nearshoring, and supply chain diversification strategies
readings: Gereffi, Global Value Chains and Development Ch. 1 (excerpt)
McKinsey, Risk, Resilience, and Rebalancing in Global Value Chains (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Global supply chains are the circulatory system of the world economy. COVID-19, geopolitical tensions, and climate change have exposed their fragility.
key_concepts: Supply chain governance: buyer-driven vs. producer-driven chains
Just-in-time vs. just-in-case inventory strategies
Supply chain risk categories: disruption, delay, price, quality
Reshoring and nearshoring: drivers and limitations
ESG requirements and supply chain due diligence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Supply chain mapping and risk assessment for a consumer electronics company

review.value: Supply chains where retailers and brand-name firms control production networks without owning factories, governing through purchasing power and standards (e.g., apparel, electronics)
The ability of a supply chain to anticipate, prepare for, respond to, and recover from disruptions, increasingly prioritized over pure cost efficiency

class_schedule.topic: International Finance for Business: Currency, Payments, and Risk
learning_objectives: Analyze how exchange rate movements affect international business
Evaluate hedging strategies for currency risk
Assess international payment systems and trade finance

lecture_notes.introduction: Currency volatility can turn a profitable international deal into a loss overnight. Managing financial risk is a core competency for international business.
key_concepts: Exchange rate determination: PPP, interest rate parity, expectations
Transaction, translation, and economic exposure
Hedging instruments: forwards, futures, options, swaps
International payment methods: letters of credit, documentary collections
Transfer pricing and international tax planning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Currency hedging exercise: designing a hedge for a company with receivables in three currencies

review.value: The risk that exchange rate changes will affect the value of a specific transaction (e.g., a receivable in foreign currency) between the time it is entered and settled
A bank guarantee that payment will be made to the exporter when documentary conditions are met, reducing credit risk in international trade

class_schedule.topic: Corporate Social Responsibility and Sustainability in International Business
learning_objectives: Evaluate CSR frameworks and their application to MNCs
Analyze the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights
Assess ESG integration as a business strategy
readings: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights

lecture_notes.introduction: International business increasingly operates under scrutiny for its social and environmental impact. CSR and ESG are no longer optional but strategic imperatives.
key_concepts: CSR evolution: philanthropy to strategic CSR to shared value
UN Guiding Principles: protect, respect, remedy framework
ESG investing and reporting: TCFD, GRI, SASB frameworks
Supply chain due diligence: EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive
Greenwashing and accountability mechanisms
lecture_notes.demonstration: ESG assessment exercise: evaluating a MNC's sustainability report against GRI standards

review.value: The authoritative framework establishing the state duty to protect human rights, the corporate responsibility to respect human rights, and access to effective remedy
The systematic inclusion of Environmental, Social, and Governance factors into investment analysis and corporate strategy, driven by both risk management and stakeholder pressure

questions.question: Explain the OLI paradigm for understanding foreign direct investment. Apply it to explain why a Japanese automaker might build a factory in the United States rather than export from Japan.
questions.answer: The OLI paradigm (Dunning) states that FDI occurs when a firm possesses: Ownership advantages (proprietary technology, brand, management skills), Location advantages (reasons to produce in the host country rather than at home), and Internalization advantages (reasons to keep production in-house rather than license). A Japanese automaker investing in the US has: O -- advanced manufacturing technology, strong brand, lean production expertise; L -- access to the large US market while avoiding tariffs and reducing shipping costs, proximity to customers, favorable exchange rate risk management; I -- need to protect proprietary production methods and maintain quality control, which licensing would jeopardize. Together, OLI explains why the firm chooses FDI over exporting (avoiding trade barriers) and over licensing (protecting technology).

rubric.key: Applies each element to the example
Shows why FDI is preferred over alternatives

questions.question: What is the Most Favored Nation (MFN) principle and why has the proliferation of regional trade agreements been seen as both complementary to and undermining of this principle?
questions.answer: MFN requires WTO members to extend any trade advantage given to one member to all members, ensuring non-discrimination. Regional trade agreements (RTAs) are permitted under GATT Article XXIV as exceptions to MFN, provided they cover substantially all trade and do not raise barriers to non-members. RTAs are complementary because they can achieve deeper integration than WTO negotiations allow and can serve as building blocks for broader liberalization. They undermine MFN because they create preferential access for members that is denied to non-members, diverting trade rather than creating it, and they fragment the global trading system into a 'spaghetti bowl' of overlapping rules that increase complexity and discrimination.

questions.question: What is transaction exposure in international business and how can a company use a forward contract to hedge it? Provide a simple example.
questions.answer: Transaction exposure is the risk that exchange rate movements will change the domestic currency value of a specific foreign-currency-denominated transaction between the time it is agreed and the time it is settled. Example: A US company sells goods to a German buyer for EUR 1 million, receivable in 90 days. If the EUR depreciates against the USD in that period, the US company receives fewer dollars than expected. To hedge, the company enters a 90-day forward contract to sell EUR 1 million at today's forward rate, locking in the dollar value regardless of future exchange rate movements. The company sacrifices potential gains if the EUR appreciates but eliminates downside risk.

questions.question: What is supply chain resilience and why has it become more important since 2020? Describe one strategy companies are using to improve resilience.
questions.answer: Supply chain resilience is the ability of a supply chain to anticipate, prepare for, respond to, and recover from disruptions while maintaining continuous operations. It has become critical since 2020 because COVID-19 exposed the fragility of just-in-time, cost-optimized global supply chains: factory shutdowns in China disrupted production worldwide, shipping bottlenecks created shortages, and single-source dependencies left companies vulnerable. Geopolitical tensions (US-China decoupling, Russia-Ukraine war) have compounded these risks. One strategy is nearshoring -- relocating production closer to end markets (e.g., US companies moving manufacturing from China to Mexico). This reduces transportation risk, shortens lead times, and provides a hedge against geopolitical disruption, though it typically increases production costs.

rubric.key: Describes a specific strategy with trade-offs

major_assignments.title: International Market Entry Strategy Report
major_assignments.description: Select a real company and develop a comprehensive market entry strategy for a new international market. Apply trade theory, country risk analysis, and entry mode frameworks to produce a professional strategy report.
objectives: Conduct systematic country and market analysis
Apply international business frameworks to strategic decisions
Develop a realistic and evidence-based entry strategy
Present strategy in a professional business format
deliverables: Country risk and opportunity assessment matrix

criteria: Thorough PESTLE analysis of target market
Accurate assessment of market size and growth
Entry mode choice justified using OLI framework
Operational plan is realistic and detailed
Currency risk identified and hedging discussed
Comprehensive market analysis with robust data
Realistic financial projections and risk assessment

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on trade, FDI, and international business concepts

participation.description: Case discussion, simulations, and team exercises

major_assignments.description: Market entry strategy report and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam on trade policy, FDI, and the business environment

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam with case-based strategic analysis

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. More than 3 unexcused absences reduce participation grade to zero.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 5% per day. No extensions within 48 hours of deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Team exercises are collaborative. All written strategy reports individually authored unless specified as group work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work original with proper citation. Fabrication of market data results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to business databases (Bloomberg, Factiva, World Bank), company filings, and trade statistics.
